summary: Problem 7 asks whether a uniform lattice Γ in a real semi-simple group containing 2-torsion can be the fundamental group of a closed manifold with ℚ-acyclic universal cover; the expected answer is 'No' via a clean Euler-characteristic obstruction. No actual proof has been submitted yet — the single tracked issue is a resolved seed/placeholder, and the 'best proof' record shows 100 open issues with no real content to evaluate. The mathematical core of the obstruction is identified but not yet written up rigorously.
problems: No substantive proof exists: the only issue is a closed seed/placeholder task tracker, not an evaluated proof.
The 'best proof' entry reports 100 open issues and an unidentified experiment, indicating no validated argument is on record.

The sketched obstruction (order-2 element acting freely on a ℚ-acyclic ~M with χ=1) is truncated mid-sentence and lacks a complete rigorous derivation, including verification of free action and the Smith-theory/Euler-characteristic step.
The solvability score (58%) reflects moderate confidence but the supporting argument has not been formalized or checked.
Hypothesis usage is unclear: it must be made explicit where the semi-simple/uniform-lattice structure (torsion-free finite-index subgroups, deck-transformation freeness) is actually needed.
highlights: A concrete, elegant strategy is identified: an order-2 deck transformation acts freely on the ℚ-acyclic universal cover whose Euler characteristic is 1, yielding a parity contradiction.
The answer direction ('No') is well-motivated and aligns with standard fixed-point/Euler-characteristic obstruction techniques.
The seed issue has already been resolved/closed, leaving a clean slate to author a fresh, focused proof.

suggested_todos.title: Write a complete proof formalizing the Euler-characteristic obstruction: ~M is ℚ-acyclic so χ(~M)=1, the order-2 element acts freely as a deck transformation, contradicting Smith theory / Lefschetz parity.
Explicitly justify that the order-2 element acts freely on ~M (deck transformations of a manifold cover are free) and that χ(~M) is well-defined and equals 1 over ℚ.
State precisely where the uniform-lattice and semi-simple hypotheses are used (e.g., existence/finiteness properties, torsion structure) versus where they are inessential.
Open a new tracked issue for the actual proof artifact and retire the stale 'best proof' record showing 100 open issues / unknown experiment.
Add a verification check via a concrete example or counter-scenario to confirm the parity argument cannot be circumvented (e.g., higher torsion, odd Euler characteristic cases).
